response:
### TCP vs UDP (Comparison)

| Feature | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (establishes a session via handshake) | Connectionless (no handshake) |
| Delivery guarantee | Reliable, ordered delivery (data is retransmitted if lost; in-order reassembly) | No delivery guarantee; may arrive out of order or not at all |
| Overhead | Higher (includes connection setup, acknowledgments, sequencing, retransmissions) | Lower (minimal headers and no reliability mechanisms) |
| Latency | Typically higher due to reliability/ordering and congestion control mechanisms | Typically lower; can send immediately without waiting for acknowledgments |
| Data ordering | Preserves order | No ordering guarantees |
| Flow / congestion control | Yes (e.g., flow control and congestion control) | No built-in flow/congestion control (application handles if needed) |
| Speed / throughput | Generally better for bulk transfer where reliability matters, but may be slower under loss | Generally faster for real-time needs; performance degrades less due to missing guarantees |
| Typical use cases | Web traffic (HTTP/HTTPS), file transfer (FTP/SFTP), email, SSH | VoIP, online gaming, DNS, streaming, real-time telemetry |
| Use when | You need correctness and completeness | You need speed and “good enough” delivery, or you handle reliability in the app |

**In short:** TCP prioritizes *reliability and order*; UDP prioritizes *speed and simplicity*.
